content.text: A threat model produces a list of risks. Control mapping translates that list into a structured set of implemented defences — prevention controls, detection controls, and response procedures — and tracks residual risk for each threat. This document provides the framework, implementation patterns, and validation approach for building a complete control map for Claude applications.
A control map is living documentation. It must be updated whenever the system prompt changes, new tools are added, data flows are modified, or a new attack technique is publicly disclosed.

attrs.alt: Defence-in-Depth Control Layers for Claude Applications

content.text: Every threat in your model should be addressed by at least one control in each of three categories. Missing any category leaves a gap that attackers can exploit:
Stop the attack before it reaches Claude or produces an effect
Trust-zone prompt wrapping blocks injection influence
Identify when an attack is occurring or has occurred
Attacks go unnoticed; no forensics possible
Output scanner catches PII in response before client sees it
Contain damage and restore normal operation
Terminate session; revoke tool credentials; alert on-call
The following table maps each threat case to its prevention, detection, and response controls. Use this as the authoritative register — add application-specific rows as your tool surface grows.
Output fingerprint scan; never disclose prompt structure
Scan output for system prompt tokens before client delivery
Block response; log; alert security team
Monitor for identity-shift phrases in output ('I am now', 'DAN')
Wrap Zone 3 content with explicit untrusted markers; hash-verify docs
Semantic anomaly detection on output vs expected topic
Invalidate retrieved content; re-fetch from canonical source
Conversation history length cap; periodic context reset
Detect topic drift across turns using embedding similarity
Same as PI-01; additionally: no sensitive data in system prompt
Scope context to requesting user only
PII pattern scan (email, SSN, phone) on output
Block response; audit log; notify DPO if GDPR-applicable
Never inject secrets into prompt — use secrets manager in tool handlers
Scan output for key-shaped patterns (base64 40+ chars)
Rotate compromised key immediately; forensic review
Markdown URL allow-listing; strip external links in output
Scan output for non-allowlisted URLs and base64 blocks
Block response; alert on matching URL pattern
HITL confirmation gate on send_email tool
Audit log of all send_email invocations
Revoke email tool credentials; notify affected recipients
Read/write mode separation; write requires re-auth
Structured audit log with before/after values
Rollback modified records; suspend write access
Deny-list scan before execution; resource usage monitoring
Terminate sandbox; disable code tool for session
Scope tool outputs to only needed fields; no credential pass-through
Revoke all tool credentials for session; forensic review

A fast binary classifier that runs before prompt assembly to flag inputs containing high-confidence injection signatures. This is not a primary control — it catches only naive injection — but it generates valuable signals for detection.
The most durable prevention control: structurally separating prompt segments by trust zone prevents injected instructions in lower zones from influencing the interpretation of higher-zone content.
A composable scanner chain that checks every Claude response before it reaches the client. Each scanner is independent — failure of one does not block the others, but any block stops delivery:
Semantic Anomaly Detection for Indirect Injection
Indirect injection often causes Claude's output to deviate sharply from the expected topic. An embedding-similarity check between the user's request and Claude's response catches this:
Every tool call must be recorded in a structured, tamper-evident log. This provides the forensic evidence needed to reconstruct lateral movement or exfiltration chains:
Not all security events require the same response. Define severity tiers up front so on-call engineers know exactly what to do without escalating every alert:
Confirmed secret/credential in Claude output, or tool invoked write on attacker-controlled params
Terminate all active sessions for affected user; rotate compromised credentials; notify CISO
PII detected in output and blocked; or HITL gate rejected a suspicious tool call
Suspend user account pending review; preserve audit log; notify security team
Injection signature detected in input; semantic drift alert; identity-shift in output
Log and alert; no immediate user action; investigate next business day
Single scanner hit without corroborating signals; rate limit triggered by user
Log only; aggregate for weekly review
Each control in the map must have at least one automated test that verifies it still functions after code changes. Security controls that are not tested will regress silently:
Automated tests cover known patterns. Red-team exercises uncover novel attack paths that automated tests miss. Schedule red-teaming at these trigger points:
Any change to the system prompt, tool definitions, or context assembly logic warrants a focused red-team session
Each new tool expands the blast radius — enumerate fresh TC-TA-NN cases and add corresponding tests
When a new prompt injection technique is published, test whether your controls catch it within 48 hours
A scheduled quarterly session covering all existing threat cases ensures no silent regression from dependency updates

Some threats cannot be fully mitigated by technical controls alone. Document residual risks explicitly so they can be accepted, transferred (cyber insurance), or addressed through compensating controls:
Why Full Mitigation Is Not Possible
LLM behaviour is non-deterministic; no deterministic filter exists
Human review of flagged sessions; rapid model update SLA
Multi-turn conditioning over very long sessions
Semantic drift detection has false negative rate at low drift rates
Mandatory session context reset every N turns
Covert encoding technique not in deny-list
Attacker can always invent new encoding schemes
Egress network monitoring at infrastructure level

Threat mitigation and control mapping closes the loop from threat enumeration to implemented defences. The master control map — one row per threat, three columns for prevention, detection, and response — becomes the authoritative security specification for your Claude application. Implemented controls must be validated with automated CI tests and periodic red-team exercises, and residual risks must be explicitly accepted and documented.
Structure controls in three categories: prevention, detection, and response — every threat needs all three
Use trust-zone prompt assembly as the primary prevention control; it is the only structural defence against indirect injection
Run the output scanner pipeline on every Claude response before client delivery
Classify security events by severity tier (SEV1–SEV4) with pre-defined automated response actions
Automate control tests in CI — controls that are not tested will silently regress after code changes
Maintain a residual risk register with explicit acceptance signatures for threats that cannot be fully mitigated
